title: Chapter 6: The Reporter
content: Anna Delgado had been a journalist for fifteen years. She'd covered corporate fraud, police misconduct, pharmaceutical scandals, and one particularly memorable story about a yoga influencer who turned out to be running a multi-level marketing scheme for essential oils that gave people chemical burns.
But she'd never received an email from inside a locked psychiatric facility.

She spent the first twenty-four hours verifying. Not the story — the story would take weeks to verify — but the basic facts. Serenity Wellness Institute existed. It was registered as a private psychiatric facility in San Mateo County. Its founder, Dr. Marcus Sterling, had a LinkedIn profile that read like a parody of wellness-industry doublespeak: *"Bridging the gap between clinical excellence and transformative patient outcomes."*
Its patient population was exclusively women under thirty. Its success rate was advertised as 94%.
And its Yelp reviews were suspicious.

Not suspicious in the obvious way — there were no one-star rants, no accusations of abuse. The reviews were all four and five stars, written in the same cadence, using the same vocabulary. *"Transformative experience." "Caring staff." "I found myself here."*
They read like they'd been written by the same person. Or the same AI.
She spent the next three days digging.

Serenity Wellness Institute had no Glassdoor page. No former employees on LinkedIn who listed it in their work history — which was strange, because a facility that size should have a staff of at least thirty. Instead, the only people who listed Serenity on their resumes were current employees. Everyone else seemed to have vanished.
Anna checked court records. Nothing. No lawsuits. No malpractice claims. No former patients suing for wrongful commitment.
That was the most suspicious thing of all.

Every psychiatric facility got sued. It was an occupational hazard of locking people up against their will. The absence of lawsuits didn't mean Serenity was clean — it meant Serenity was very, very good at making sure no one ever filed one.
She pulled up the sender's email again. *Chloe.* No last name. A twenty-three-year-old committed by her parents for posting a TikTok.
Anna searched "Chloe TikTok venture capitalist father."

The video came up immediately. Five-point-two million views. A girl with messy bleach-blonde hair and dark roots, sitting in what looked like a Brooklyn apartment, speaking directly to the camera with a dead-eyed calm that was somehow more devastating than rage.

*"My father is a venture capitalist. Which means he invests in companies that extract value from human misery and calls it disruption. Last quarter, his portfolio included a telehealth startup that overprescribed opioids, a gig-work platform that classified employees as contractors to avoid benefits, and an AI hiring tool that systematically rejected Black applicants.*
*"He makes eight figures a year. He has a Tesla. He has a second Tesla for weekends. And he has a daughter who won't stop talking about it.*
*"Hi, Dad."*
The first external inquiry reached Serenity Wellness Institute at 9:47 AM on day eight.
Dr. Sterling was in his office, reviewing patient compliance metrics — the spreadsheet he used to track which girls were progressing and which needed "adjusted protocols" — when his desk phone rang.
"Dr. Sterling," his assistant said, "there's a reporter on the line. From The Verge. She's asking about patient admission criteria."
Sterling's hand stopped moving. The cursor on his spreadsheet hovered over a cell labeled *CHEN, C. — ESCALATING.*
"What did you tell her?"
"Nothing, per policy. But she was very... persistent. She said she'd call back."

Sterling looked at the spreadsheet. At the patient labeled *ESCALATING.* At the new night guard he'd been forced to hire because Rodriguez — useless, phone-addicted Rodriguez — had apparently been too incompetent to notice something was happening under his watch.
He didn't know what, exactly. But the reporter's call confirmed it.
Something was happening.
"Get Holloway," Sterling said. "Now."
In Room 214, Chloe was journaling.
She'd promised Holloway she'd engage with the therapeutic prompts. And she was — just not the way Holloway expected. The prompt on the whiteboard that morning was: *What does healing mean to you?*
Chloe wrote:
*Healing means getting out of here and burning this place to the ground. Metaphorically. Probably.*
She underlined *probably.*

Then she smiled at the camera, because she'd learned something important in the last eight days: the camera wasn't watching her. She was watching the camera. Every blink was a heartbeat. Every heartbeat was a tell.
And the heartbeats, today, were faster than they'd been all week.
They were scared.
Good.
